coredata.dc:title: Pb2+-modified graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for biological aminothiols mediated by an inner filter effect
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Dajun
Zhang, Xiaomeng
Qi, Yanxia
Ding, Shushu
Cao, Sumei
Zhu, Anwei
Shi, Guoyue
coredata.dc:description: A simple, yet efficient fluorescent method for detecting biological aminothiols has been developed based on the inner filter effect principle that utilizes graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the donor and aminothiol-Pb2+ complex as the absorber. Well-defined diethanol amine modified graphene quantum dots (GQD-DEA) were first synthesized by a “synthesis-modification integration” strategy. Then, the addition of aminothiols can bind with Pb2+ and displace it from the surface of preformed GQD-DEA-Pb2+, leading to the formation of aminothiol-Pb2+ complex. Due to the complementary overlap between the excitation band of GQD-DEA and the absorption band of aminothiol-Pb2+ complex, the fluorescence of GQDs was quenched, thereby a turn-off fluorescent assay for the determination of aminothiols via the inner filter effect was constructed. This strategy enabled cost-effective and selective detection of aminothiols with theoretical simplicity and low technical demands. Moreover, the fluorescent probe offered high selectivity for aminothiol due to the strong binding of aminothiol with Pb2+ in comparison with other amino acids and the inner filter effect provided by thiol-Pb2+ complex. Under the optimum conditions, the linear concentration ranges were 5×10−5–6×10−4 M for cysteine, 5×10−5–1×10−3 M for homocysteine, 1×10−4–2×10−3 M for glutathione, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aminothiols
Graphene quantum dots
Pb2+
Inner filter effect
Synthesis-modification integration

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of selective aminothiol detection by GQD-DEA-Pb2+
Detection of aminothiols in blood serum

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21405048 ) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2014M550225 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic for the preparation of GQD-DEA, (b) Schematic illustration for biosensing of cysteine based on GQD-DEA-Pb2+.
FT-IR spectra of as-prepared (a) GO, (b) GO-Cl and (c) GQD-DEA.

(a,b) TEM images of the GQD-DEA with different magnifications. (c) The size distribution of GQD-DEA. (d) AFM topography image of GQD-DEA on a silicon substrate, with (e) the height profile along the line in the topographic image. (f) The height distribution of GQD-DEA.

(a) Normalized UV-vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of GQD-DEA. (b) Absorption spectra of aminothiols and their complex with Pb2+. (c) Absorption spectra of GQD-DEA-Pb2+ in the absence and presence of Cys, Hcy, and GSH.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQD-DEA-Pb2+ in the absence (black curve) and presence (red curve) of cysteine. Inset: fluorescence emission spectra of GQD-DEA in the absence (black curve) and presence (red curve) of cysteine. (b) Plot of (Fo-F)/Fo at 407nm as a function of cysteine concentration obtained with 0.02mgmL−1 GQD-DEA-Pb2+. (Fo-F)/Fo stands for the ratio of fluorescence changes of GQD-DEA-Pb2+ upon addition of cysteine and the initial fluorescence of GQD-DEA-Pb2+. Error bars represent standard error measurements (s.e.m.). The inset of (b) shows the linear plot of (Fo-F)/Fo at 407nm versus the concentration of cysteine ranging from 5×10−5–6×10−4 M. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence responses of GQD-DEA-Pb2+ towards various (a) amino acids (1mM) and (b) metal ions (1mM for Na+, K+ and Ca2+, and 10μM for other metal ions). (Fo-F)/Fo stands for the ratio of fluorescence changes of GQD-DEA-Pb2+ induced by amino acid and the initial fluorescence emission of GQD-DEA-Pb2+. Error bars represent standard error measurements (s.e.m.).
Effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence of GQD-DEA-Pb2+.
Recovery of aminothiols added to 400-fold diluted blood serum sample.

head.ce:title: Pb2+-modified graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for biological aminothiols mediated by an inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China Normal University, 500 Dongchuan Road, Shanghai 200241, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, yet efficient fluorescent method for detecting biological aminothiols has been developed based on the inner filter effect principle that utilizes graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the donor and aminothiol-Pb2+ complex as the absorber. Well-defined diethanol amine modified graphene quantum dots (GQD-DEA) were first synthesized by a “synthesis-modification integration” strategy. Then, the addition of aminothiols can bind with Pb2+ and displace it from the surface of preformed GQD-DEA-Pb2+, leading to the formation of aminothiol-Pb2+ complex. Due to the complementary overlap between the excitation band of GQD-DEA and the absorption band of aminothiol-Pb2+ complex, the fluorescence of GQDs was quenched, thereby a turn-off fluorescent assay for the determination of aminothiols via the inner filter effect was constructed. This strategy enabled cost-effective and selective detection of aminothiols with theoretical simplicity and low technical demands. Moreover, the fluorescent probe offered high selectivity for aminothiol due to the strong binding of aminothiol with Pb2+ in comparison with other amino acids and the inner filter effect provided by thiol-Pb2+ complex. Under the optimum conditions, the linear concentration ranges were 5×10−5–6×10−4 M for cysteine, 5×10−5–1×10−3 M for homocysteine, 1×10−4–2×10−3 M for glutathione, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Biological aminothiols, such as cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), and glutathione (GSH), play a critical role in cellular functions, protein synthesis, detoxification and metabolism [1–3]. Besides this, aminothiols concentration in biological fluids is of importance since altered levels of aminothiols have been implicated in a number of pathological conditions including Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s disease, as well as autoimmune deficiency syndrome [4–6]. Consequently, great efforts have been devoted to the development of analytical methods for the detection of aminothiols, including chromatography [7], electrochemistry [8,9], mass spectrometry [10], colorimetry and fluorescence method [11–19]. Among the various analytical methods available, fluorescent assay has its own advantages over other techniques in its high sensitivity and simplicity for implementation. However, it remains a challenging work to create a fluorescent probe that possesses an appropriate combination of facile synthesis, low cost, high selectivity and photostability [20–22].

Inner filter effect (IFE)-based fluorescent assay has gained increasing interest for applications in the detection of environmentally or biologically important analytes [23–31]. The IFE, which results from the absorption of the excitation and/or emission light of fluorophores by absorbers, does not require the linking between the absorbers and the fluorophores, making the IFE-based fluorescent assay comparatively simple and flexible. Moreover, an improved detection sensitivity may be obtained compared with the absorbance or fluorescence intensity alone because the varying absorbance of an absorber results in exponential variance in the luminescence of fluorophores. With the advantages of high extinction coefficient and tunable surface plasmon resonance absorption, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been frequently adopted as an absorber unit in the IFE-based detection system [23,24,27–31]. However, the surface plasmon resonance of AuNPs is very sensitive to dielectric constants of both the metal and the surrounding medium [32,33]. Without rational design of the surface chemistry of AuNPs, they often tend to aggregate in complex samples to generate false positive signals, due to the interference from salt [34], metal ions [35], small molecules [36], etc.

Very recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which possess favourable attributes of simple synthesis, low cost, and good chemical/photo stability, emerge as an attractive alternative to semiconductor quantum dots and organic fluorophore for fluorescent imaging and sensing [37–39]. However, no research about applications of GQDs as fluorophore in the IFE-based fluorescent assay has been reported so far. On the other hand, GQDs-based sensors are routinely fabricated by at least two steps, namely GQDs synthesis and surface functionalization by conjugation technique. Indubitably, the conjugation process is often tedious and uncontrollable for such small sized GQDs (often less than 10nm), and even detrimental to their fluorescence. Therefore, a GQDs-based sensing platform, which gets rid of surface modification, will exhibit great promise in the development of simple but effective fluorescent sensor.

In this work, we first present a “synthesis-modification integration” strategy for facile fabrication of diethanol amine (DEA)-functionalized GQDs (GQD-DEA), avoiding tedious and uncontrollable surface modification. Then, by exploiting the competition between DEA and aminothiols for Pb2+and the complementary overlap between the excitation band of GQDs and the absorption band of aminothiol-Pb2+ complex, an IFE-based fluorescent assay for aminothiols was constructed. Such sensing platform would greatly decrease the assay cost, simplify assay procedure, exhibit stability against high salt solution, and overcome potential interferences in body fluids. On this basis, an effective sensing method based on the designed GQD probe was further developed for the detection of aminothiols in blood serum. Moreover, to the best of our knowledge, this is the first application of GQDs as fluorophore in the IFE-based fluorescent assay.

Diethanol amine (DEA), alanine (Ala), asparaginate (Asn), aspartic acid (Asp), arginine (Arg), cysteine (Cys), glutamate (Glu), glutamine (Gln), glutathione (GSH), glycine (Gly), histidine (His), isoleucine (Ile), leucine (Leu), lysine (Lys), methionine (Met), phenylalanine (Phe), proline (Pro), serine (Ser), threonine (Thr), tryptophan (Trp), tyrosine (Tyr) and Valine (Val) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All other reagents were of analytical grade and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Aqueous solutions were prepared from deionized water (18MΩcm, Hitech science tool laboratory water purification system).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained with a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope. Infrared (IR) spectrum was taken on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 spectrometer. Fluorescence emission spectra was measured on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer with a 150W xenon lamp. UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded by a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer. Atomic force microscope (AFM) images were recorded by tapping mode on the Multimode 8 AFM from Bruker.

The oxidation of graphite was performed by the modification of Hummers method [40,41]. First, graphite powder (0.5g), NaNO3 (0.5g) and concentrated H2SO4 (23mL) were mixed and stirred in an ice bath for several minutes. Then, 3g of KMnO4 was slowly added, and the solution was transferred to a 35°C oil bath and kept stirring for 2h. Next, 40mL of water was slowly added, and the solution was stirred for 30min while the temperature was raised to 90±5°C. After that, 100mL of water was added, followed by the slow addition of H2O2 (3mL, 30%), turning the colour of the solution from dark brown to yellow. Then, the warm solution was filtered and washed with 1M HCl (200mL) and water (200mL) respectively for two times. Finally, the obtained brown solid was dried under 60°C for 24h.

First, 0.1g of GO was dispersed in 10mL of SOCl2 and 2mL of toluene. The mixture was stirred at 70°C under reflux for 24h. Then, the obtained black solid (GO-Cl) was washed with ultra-dried tetrahydrofuran (THF) two times and dried at 60°C for 24h. Next, 30mg of GO-Cl and large excess of DEA were added into 10mL of DMF followed by the addition of 0.5mL of triethylamine (TEA). The mixture was stirred at 120°C under reflux for 72h to obtain GO-DEA [42]. After the solution was cooled to room temperature, the excess of DEA and other impurities were removed by two cycles of centrifugation and washing with DMF. Finally, the precipitate was washed with a small quantity of H2O to remove Et3N·HCl.

GQD-DEA are obtained by one-step hydrothermal method [39,43], using GO-DEA as the sole precursor. 15mg of GO-DEA was first dispersed in 12mL of water. Then, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200°C for 24h. After the reaction was complete, the reactor was cooled to room temperature. Next, the product was filtered to get light yellow transparent solution and black precipitates were discarded. Lastly, the solid samples can be obtained by evaporating the solvents.

A typical fluorescent analysis was performed by the following steps. The stock solution of 0.1M aminothiols were freshly prepared. Then, 1mL of various concentration of aminothiols were added to 1mL of 0.04mgmL−1 GQD-DEA-Pb2+ and allowed to react for 1min. Subsequently, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture were obtained in the wavelength range of 360–550nm with an excitation at 300nm and maximum emission at 407nm. The slit widths of the excitation and emission were both 5.0nm.

As a starting point of our study, GO was synthesized by a modified Hummers method. The Fourier transforms infrared (FT-IR) spectrum is given in Fig. 1 . The band at 1633cm−1 corresponds to the CC stretching modes of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. The band at around 1073cm−1 indicates the existence of COH group, whereas the bands at around 1736cm−1 and 1389cm−1 are due to carbonyl (CO) and CO of carboxylic groups. The band at around 3422cm−1 corresponds to the OH stretching mode. These data demonstrate that the as-prepared GO are surrounded by COOH and/or OH groups [20–22]. Then, GQD-DEA was synthesized from GO by acyl chlorination and amidation reactions, followed by the hydrothermal treatment to cut the GO-DEA (as shown in Scheme 1 a). The reaction between COOH and SOCl2 was identified through the disappearance of the band at 1389cm−1. Besides, four peaks were clearly observed at 3335 (νN-H), 664 (νN-H), 1657 (Amide I, CO) and 1538 (Amide II, N-H and C-N) cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra of GQD-DEA, which suggested that carboxyl groups of GO had been converted into amide groups after acyl chlorination and amidation reactions due to the attachment of DEA onto the surface of GO and the subsequent hydrothermal treatment did not destroy the amide groups on the surface of GQD-DEA [20–22,37]. The as-obtained GQD-DEA can freely disperse in water with transparent appearance without further ultrasonic dispersion (Fig. S1). Typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 2 a and b) show that the as-synthesized GQD-DEA are monodispersed with an average size of around 1.8nm (Fig. 2c). The corresponding atomic force microscopy (AFM) images (Fig. 2d and e) reveal a typical topographic height of 0.5–2.5nm (Fig. 2f), suggesting that the average thickness of GQD-DEA is 1.3nm. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the UV–vis absorption peak of GQD-DEA at 260nm represents a typical absorption of an aromatic π system [20–22,44]. The emission wavelength of the GQD-DEA is excitation-independent when the excitation wavelength is shorter than 300nm, but excitation-dependent when the excitation wavelength is longer than 300nm (Fig. S2). Meanwhile, when the excitation wavelength is shorter or longer than 300nm, Raman scattering peak or double frequency scattering peaks are included in the emission spectrum and form a shoulder peak which would cause potential interference in the subsequent experiment. So for the sake of avoiding the interference, we chose 300nm as the best excitation wavelength for further investigation.

Specific metal-ligand interactions, such as the strong binding preference of thiolate group toward Pb2+ by the formation of Pb2+–S bonds, have been utilized in the development of aminothiol-selective sensors [8,45]. Inspired by such interactions, Pb2+ was incorporated into the GQD-DEA through the binding affinity of DEA towards transition metal ions [45,46]. Furthermore, aminothiol-Pb2+ complexes were found to have characteristic absorption peak at ∼269nm, as shown in Fig. 3b. Thus, by virtue of the complementary overlap between the excitation band of GQD-DEA (300nm) and the absorption band (250–330nm) of aminothiol-Pb2+ complex, Pb2+-modified graphene quantum dots have great potential to act as a fluorescent probe for aminothiols mediated by an inner filter effect (IFE).

To prove the working principle, the response of GQD-DEA-Pb2+ towards aminothiols (such as Cys) was then determined. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was decreased upon addition of Cys to the solution of GQD-DEA-Pb2+, whereas no obvious change in the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was observed after the addition of Cys to the aqueous GQD-DEA solution without Pb2+ (inset of Fig. 4a). On the other hand, upon addition of Cys to the solution of GQD-DEA-Pb2+, the characteristic absorption peak at 269nm was enhanced (Fig. 3c), while the absorption spectrum of GQD-DEA did not change in the presence of Cys (data not shown), implying no complex formation between GQD-DEA and Cys. Obviously, the increase of the absorbance at 250–330nm resulted from the formation of Cys-Pb2+ complex (Fig. 3b and c). Due to the complementary overlap between the absorption band of Cys-Pb2+ complex and the excitation wavelength at 300nm, the fluorescence of GQD-DEA-Pb2+ was thus quenched through the IFE of Cys-Pb2+ complex on the fluorescence of GQDs. This IFE-based fluorescence probe can detect Cys in the range of 5×10−5–6×10−4 M (Fig. 4b) with a detection limit as low as 1×10−5 M (based on a signal-to-noise ratio of S/N=3).

Highly selective detection of aminothiols in biological fluids is of great significance for understanding their roles involved in the physiological and pathological events. The selectivity of the fluorescent probe was evaluated by testing the response of the probe to other α-amino acids (Fig. 5 a). It can be seen that other amino acids without thiolate group did not interfere with the fluorescent response of GQD-DEA-Pb2+ towards Cys. In addition, the fluorescent probe did not show obvious fluorescence changes in response to common metal ions that coexist in body fluids (Fig. 5b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of selective aminothiol detection by GQD-DEA-Pb2+
ce:section.ce:para: The selective amino acid response of GQD-DEA-Pb2+ is correlated to the relative formation constants of the complexes formed by Pb2+ ion with different amino acids [8,45]. Besides, only when the absorption band of Pb2+-amino acid complex possesses the complementary overlap with the excitation band of the GQDs, the inner filter effect occurs leading to the quenching of GQDs fluorescence. Thus, the selective fluorescence quenching could be rationalized by the competitive Pb2+ binding between GQD-DEA and amino acids present in the solution and the complementary overlap between the absorption band of the amino acids-Pb2+ complex and the excitation band of the GQDs. The formation constant of complexes formed by Pb2+ with DEA is lower than that of Pb2+ complex of aminothiols [46–48]. Therefore, we proposed that the Pb2+ ions were preferentially displaced from the surface of GQD-DEA upon the binding of the thiolate group of aminothiols. In addition, the binding of Pb2+ with aminothiols created new absorption band which covered the excitation band of the GQDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching (as shown in Scheme 1b). Therefore, the above method was also employed to determine the concentration of Hcy and GSH. The linear concentration range is 5×10−5–1×10−3 M for Hcy and 1×10−4–2×10−3 M for GSH, respectively.

Besides, we also studied the influences of ionic strength on the stability of GQD-DEA-Pb2+. The results demonstrated that no significant changes of the fluorescence response were observed in solutions with different ionic strength (0–12M KCl aqueous solution, Fig. 6 ). Compared with common fluorescence dye and AuNPs [34,49]. GQD-DEA-Pb2+ shows better stability against high salt solutions. Therefore, GQD-DEA has the potential to be used as a fluorescent probe for determination of amino acid in complex environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of aminothiols in blood serum
ce:section.ce:para: Taking the results above, the advantages of facile synthesis, low cost, high selectivity and photostability of GQD-DEA-Pb2+ substantially make it superior to other dyes and AuNPs in the development of an IFE-based fluorescent assay for determination of aminothiols in pharmaceutical samples. In this regard, experments were performed to determine the feasibility of using GQD-DEA-Pb2+ to measure aminothiols in human blood serum. Validation of the proposed method was performed by examining the recoveries of the spike serum samples. Results of the recovery studies are summarized in Table 1 . The good recovery results (96–104%) for aminothiols in the spike serum samples indicated that the constituents in the blood serum had minor effect on the determination of aminothiol. Thus, the designed sensor has great potential to be used for determination of aminothiol in body fluids from patients involving a dysregulation in the redox balance without the need for any preseparation method.

In summary, by facilely preparing GQDs-DEA as IFE fluorophores and designing aminothiol-Pb2+ as absorbers, we have developed a low cost, effective, and selective strategy for the fluorescent detection of aminothiols. The fluorescent probe shows good photostability against high salt solutions, thus successfully applied for sensing aminothiols in spike blood serum. This work also provides a methodology to design GQDs-based fluorescent assays with high specificity for biological species, which may be related to physiological and pathological events.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21405048) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2014M550225).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.05.103.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cysteine-mediated redox signaling: chemistry, biology, and tools for discovery
A colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent probe for quantitative detection of GSH at physiologically relevant levels
Expanding the functional diversity of proteins through cysteine oxidation
Plasma homocysteine as a risk factor for dementia and Alzheimer's disease
Pyruvate released by astrocytes protects neurons from copper-catalyzed cysteine neurotoxicity
Status of glutathione and other thiols and disulfides in human plasma

Simultaneous determination of several amino acids including homocysteine, cysteine and glutamic acid, in human plasma by isocratic reversed-phase high-performance liquid chromatography with fluorimetric detection
Lead phthalocyanine as a selective carrier for preparation of a cysteine-selective electrode
Synthesis and assembly of ultrathin film of Ni(OH)2 nanoparticles at gas/liquid interface, its high electrocatalytical oxidation toward bio-thiols and selective determination of cysteine
Study of highly selective and efficient thiol derivatization using selenium reagents by mass spectrometry
Recent progress in fluorescent and colorimetric chemosensors for detection of amino acids
Recent progress in luminescent and colorimetric chemosensors for detection of thiols
A highly selective fluorescence turn-on sensor for cysteine/homocysteine and its application in bioimaging
BODIPY-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for highly selective detection of glutathione over cysteine and homocysteine
A highly sensitive near-infrared fluorescent probe for cysteine and homocysteine in living cells
A dual emission fluorescent probe enables simultaneous detection of glutathione and cysteine/homocysteine
Selective fluorescence detection of cysteine over homocysteine and glutathione based on a cysteine-triggered dual Michael addition/retro-aza-aldol cascade reaction
Photostable red turn-on fluorescent diketopyrrolopyrrole chemodosimeters for the detection of cysteine in living cells
Discriminative fluorescence sensing of biothiols in vitro and in living cells
Carbon-dot-based dual-emission nanohybrid produces a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for in vivo imaging of cellular copper ions
Development of a carbon quantum dots-based fluorescent Cu2+ probe suitable for living cell imaging
A two-photon turn-on fluorescent probe based on carbon nanodots for imaging and selective biosensing of hydrogen sulfide in live cells and tissues
Design of fluorescent assays for cyanide and hydrogen peroxide based on the inner filter effect of metal nanoparticles
Homogeneous fluorescence-based immunoassay via inner filter effect of gold nanoparticles on fluorescence of CdTe quantum dots
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
On-off-on fluorescent carbon dot nanosensor for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on the inner filter effect
Highly sensitive detection of Caspase-3 activities via a nonconjugated gold nanoparticle-quantum dot pair mediated by an inner-filter effect
Inner filter effect of gold nanoparticles on the fluorescence of rare-earth phosphate nanocrystals and its application for determination of biological aminothiols
Gold nanocluster-assisted fluorescent detection for hydrogen peroxide and cholesterol based on the inner filter effect of gold nanoparticles
Colorimetric and phosphorimetric dual-signaling strategy mediated by inner filter effect for highly sensitive assay of organophosphorus pesticides
A selective sensor for cyanide ion (CN−) based on the inner filter effect of metal nanoparticles with photoluminescent carbon dots as the fluorophore
Review of some interesting surface plasmon resonance-enhanced properties of noble metal nanoparticles and their applications to biosystems
Tailoring surface plasmons through the morphology and assembly of metal nanoparticles
Colorimetric detection of glucose in rat brain using gold nanoparticles
Gold nanoparticle-based sensing of spectroscopically silent heavy metal ions
Colorimetric detection of DNA small molecules, proteins, and ions using unmodified gold nanoparticles and conjugated polyelectrolytes
A two-photon ratiometric fluorescence probe for Cupric Ions in Live Cells and Tissues
Highly sensitive and selective fluorescent detection of cerebral lead (II) based on graphene quantum dot conjugates
A novel composite of graphene quantum dots and molecularly imprinted polymer for fluorescent detection of paranitrophenol
Langmuir-blodgett assembly of graphite oxide single layers
Porphyrin and fullerene covalently functionalized graphene hybrid materials with large nonlinear optical properties
Hydrothermal route for cutting graphene sheets into blue-luminescent graphene quantum dots
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
Dithiocarbamate-capped silver nanoparticles as a resonance light scattering probe for simultaneous detection of lead (II) ions and cysteine
Selective and efficient magnetic separation of Pb2+ via gold nanoparticle-based visual binding enrichment
Ultrasensitive Pb2+ detection by glutathione-capped quantum dots
Some metal complexes of sulfur-containing amino acids
On the quencher concentration dependence of fluorescence quenching: the role of solution dielectric constant and ionic strength

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dajun Yu received her M.S. degree in 2013 at East China Normal University (China). Her research interest is fluorescent sensors and molecular imprinting.
Xiaomeng Zhang is presently doing M.S. degree in analytical chemistry at East China Normal University (China). Her research interest is fluorescence-based chemosensors.

Yanxia Qi is currently pursuing Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry at East China Normal University (China). Her main research interest is fluorescent chemosensing of biologically relevant metal ions.

Shushu Ding is currently a Ph.D. candidate in analytical chemistry at East China Normal University under the guidance of Prof. Guoyue Shi and Anwei Zhu. Her research interests focus on development of new chemosensors.

Sumei Cao got her B.S. degree of chemistry (2014) at Nanjing Normal University (China). Subsequently, she joined Prof. Guoyue Shi’s group as a master student at East China Normal University (China). Her research interests are in the areas of electroanalytical chemistry and chemical sensors.

Anwei Zhu received his Ph.D. degree (2013) in analytical chemistry from Department of Chemistry at Tongji University (China). Subsequently, he joined the School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering at East China Normal University (China) as a lecture. His research interests include nano-optical probe and ultra-microelectrode.

Guoyue Shi received his Ph.D. degree (1999) from East China Normal University (China). He acted as an advanced visiting scholar in Laboratory of Research and Development, BAS Inc., Tokyo, Japan from January 2002 to January 2003 and had a postdoc position in University of Pittsburgh, PA, USA from March 2003 to May 2005. He is now a professor in School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering at East China Normal University. His research interests cover biosensors and analytical chemistry.
